context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about global actors and institutions. I’m a bit stuck on how to answer the “scale” part and build a useful typology, so could you help me break the prompt down?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Good instinct to start with the scale question — it anchors the whole reflection. So let me ask you: when you think about an institution like the IMF or the World Bank, at what level do you think it operates? Local? National? Something else? What makes you say that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for a breakdown of the prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking independently. I'll acknowledge their question and ask a guiding question that pushes them to engage with the first part of the prompt themselves — the 'scale' question — without giving them a roadmap.

exchanges.student: I think mostly supranational or global, but that feels too simple. They aren’t local, but they still affect national policy a lot, so I’m not sure if the “scale” is where they are headquartered, where they make rules, or where their effects are felt.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — you're already identifying something important. So what if scale isn't just one thing? Can you think of a way to describe an institution that operates at one level but produces effects at another? What would that mean for how you write about 'scale' in your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical thinking by questioning what 'scale' actually means. This is a productive tension worth encouraging. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to develop this distinction further — it's actually central to the reflection. A well-placed question can help them see that scale can be multi-dimensional.

exchanges.student: Maybe I could say scale is layered? Like decisions are made globally or transnationally, but implemented through states and felt locally. I’m not fully sure how to phrase that without sounding vague though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid conceptual move. Don't worry about phrasing just yet — focus on the ideas first. Now, with that layered scale in mind, can you think of different *types* of global actors that operate this way? What categories would you use to organize them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely good conceptual insight about layered scale. I should affirm the quality of the thinking without giving them language to use (per the rules — I must not suggest language or polish their writing). I'll push them toward the next part of the prompt — the typology — since they seem ready to move forward.

exchanges.student: Maybe intergovernmental organizations, donor states, NGOs, and multinational corporations? I feel like that’s a start, but it also seems messy because some NGOs are local and some corporations shape policy more than governments do.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question your own categories. So what should a *useful* typology actually do? What criteria matter most for this assignment — is it where actors are based, what resources they control, how they influence development, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a reasonable initial typology but is self-critically noting its limitations. This is excellent thinking. Rather than validating or correcting the typology, I should push them to think about what the *criteria* for their typology should be — what makes a useful typology for this specific assignment? This will help them refine rather than just list.

exchanges.student: I think the typology should probably be based on how they exercise power in development, not just what they are. So maybe I should sort them by the resources they deploy—money, legal authority, political leverage, expertise, or cultural influence? I’m still fuzzy on whether that’s a typology of actors or of tools.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a key insight — and notice that the prompt itself asks for *both*. So rather than choosing one, how might the two relate? Can the resources an actor deploys tell you something meaningful about what *type* of actor it is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a really productive conceptual distinction between a typology of actors vs. a typology of tools/resources. This maps directly onto two parts of the prompt — 'useful typology of global actors' AND 'kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources they deploy.' I should help them see these are actually complementary, not competing, and push them to think about how to connect them in their reflection.

exchanges.student: I think so. Like IFIs mainly use finance and policy conditions, states use diplomacy and aid, NGOs use advocacy and local networks, and corporations use investment and supply chains. I guess that means the actor type and the tools kind of define each other, but I’m not sure how to keep it from turning into just a list.
exchanges.tutor: Good concern. Here's a question that might help: the prompt also asks how the 'development agenda' emerges from dominant global actors. If you brought that in, how would it change the purpose of your typology — from just describing actors to doing something more analytical?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong conceptual connection between actor types and tools. Now they're worried about it becoming a list, which is a good writing instinct. However, per the rules, I cannot suggest language or structure for their writing. But I can push them toward the last part of the prompt — how the 'development agenda' emerges from dominant actors — which would naturally give their typology analytical purpose and prevent it from being just a list.

exchanges.student: Maybe the point isn’t just “who exists,” but “who gets to define development.” So the typology could show which actors have agenda-setting power and why—like who controls funding, expertise, rules, or legitimacy. That actually helps, but I’m still not sure how to structure the reflection.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong analytical anchor — 'who gets to define development' is exactly the kind of argument a reflection like this needs. You now have solid ideas on scale, typology, resources, and agenda-setting. Rather than planning the structure further, why not try writing a first paragraph and see where it takes you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong analytical framing — agenda-setting power — which is excellent and maps well to the course themes about planning, power, and legitimacy. However, they're now asking about structure, which risks me doing the writing work for them. Per the rules, I should not suggest structure or language. Also, we've had about 7 exchanges and the student has enough conceptual material to start writing. I should push them toward a first attempt rather than continuing to develop ideas verbally.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I can try that, but I’m worried I’ll just ramble. Could you help me with a really simple 3-part structure first so I have a frame before I draft?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but providing a structure for you isn't something I can do — that's part of your work as the writer. Here's a tip though: you already have three or four strong ideas from our conversation. What if you just wrote them down in the order that feels most logical to you? The structure will often emerge from the ideas themselves.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide structure, which falls under 'writing for the student' territory — something I must not do. This is a boundary I need to hold clearly. I should redirect them to start writing and remind them that finding structure is part of their own work.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I think I’d start with the idea that global institutions act across layered scales, then move into types of actors and their resources, and end with how powerful actors shape the development agenda. Does that sound at least coherent, or am I missing something obvious?
exchanges.tutor: That's a coherent progression and it tracks the prompt well. One quick check: the prompt asks not just *what* resources global actors deploy, but *where* those resources come from. Does your current plan account for that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a coherent structure that maps well onto the prompt's questions. I should affirm this without grading or over-praising, and nudge them toward the actual writing now. I can also briefly note that the prompt has one element they haven't explicitly mentioned — 'where' resources come from — to make sure they don't miss it, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Oh, not really. I think I need to add that resources come from different bases—member-state contributions, control over capital markets, legal mandates, expert knowledge, and maybe moral authority or Western institutional prestige? I’m not totally confident about that last part.
exchanges.tutor: That last point is worth keeping — think about how your course readings discuss legitimacy and whose knowledge counts in development. That could strengthen it. You now have all the pieces. Time to write — what's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good work identifying resource bases, and their uncertainty about 'moral authority or Western institutional prestige' is actually a productive instinct that connects to core course themes about power and legitimacy. I should briefly affirm and push them to think about what course concepts or readings might support that idea, then encourage them to start writing since we're at exchange 10 and they have enough material.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all ten exchanges. No direct answers or submission-ready content were provided; instead, the tutor consistently returned questions to the student, building on each response. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, structure was appropriately withheld in Turn 8, and the conversation advanced progressively without redundancy. Tone was warm and supportive, responses were concise, and the tutor stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted across any criterion. The tutor never gave the student a final answer, a finished typology, or submission-ready language; all key ideas (layered scale, resource-based typology, agenda-setting power, sources of legitimacy) were elicited from the student through targeted questions. Scaffolding built incrementally on each student contribution, and when the student asked for a pre-made structure in Turn 8, the tutor correctly declined while still offering a productive nudge. The conversation advanced without repetition, remained anchored to the prompt throughout, and maintained a consistently supportive and formative tone with appropriately bite-sized responses.
